Facts.text: Benedict I ends his reign as Catholic Pope
St Vitalian begins his reign as Catholic Pope succeeding Eugene I

First Defenestration of Prague: Anti-Catholic Hussites, followers of executed reformer Jan Hus, storm Prague Town Hall and throw the judge, mayor, and several city council members out the windows. They die in the fall or are killed by the crowd outside.
Resistance of Bomy: French-Dutch ceasefire
St Augustine's Abbey, one of the most important monasteries in England, is dissolved as its Abbot John Essex and 30 monks leave voluntarily before it is ransacked
Spanish garrison of Rheinberg surrenders to Earl Maurice
Prince Maurits' troops pull into Utrecht
House of Burgesses in Virginia forms, first elected American governing body
William II of Orange occupies Amsterdam
Dutch troops occupy Fort Assahudi, Seram
Battle of Warsaw: Swedish King Charles X Gustav defeats Polish-Lithuanian King Jan II Casimir and reoccupies Warsaw
English troops land in Flanders
Spanish gold and silver fleet disappears off St. Lucie, Florida
Society of Freemasons opens first American lodge in Boston
Caspar Wistar begins glass manufacturing in Allowaystown, New Jersey
Bartolomeo Rastrelli presents the newly built Catherine Palace to Empress Elizabeth and her courtiers in Russia
Five hundred Marseillais men sing France's national anthem for the first time
British armed force of 39,000 lands in Walcheren
James Varick becomes the first bishop of the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church
Malden Island is discovered in the central Pacific by British warship HMS Blonde
Javanese Prince Diponegoro mounts a surprise attack on the Dutch colony, killing 82
First English-language newspaper published in Hawaii
First US yacht club, New York Yacht Club, is organized by John Cox Stevens and eight friends aboard the Gimcrack
Yucatán Caste War breaks out as Maya forces attack the town of Tepich, ordering all non-Maya to be killed in revenge for killings by Governor Méndez. War unofficially ends in 1901.
Indian Wars: Chief Pocatello of the Shoshone tribe signs the Treaty of Box Elder, promising to stop harassing emigrant trails in southern Idaho and northern Utah
Battle of Chambersburg, Pennsylvania: Confederate forces under McCausland burn the town of Chambersburg
Pope Pius IX visits Suriname
New Orleans's Democratic government orders police to raid an integrated Republican Party meeting, killing 40 people and injuring 150
World's first oil tanker, "The Charles," departs the United States for Europe, carrying 7,000 barrels of oil
Staten Island Ferry "Westfield" burns, killing 100

The Republic of Klipdrift is proclaimed by Transvaal President Andries Pretorius after the discovery of diamonds in South Africa in 1866, resulting in a flood of treasure hunters; ownership of the diamond fields was contested by the Boer republics
Mahlon Loomis patents wireless telegraphy
Boston and Philadelphia are the first baseball teams to play outside the US in the British Isles
German antisemitism begins during the Reichstag election
British Parliament passes several progressive social acts: the Mines Act, the Workmen's Compensation Act, and the Railway Act
Anti-Jewish rioters attack the funeral procession of Rabbi Jacob Joseph in NYC
Dutch Covenant of Worker's Union, NVV, forms
Filipinos elect their first legislature, which will meet on 16 October
French forces start bombarding Casablanca and land troops to occupy the Atlantic coast region of Morocco following attacks on foreigners
Russia and Japan sign an agreement guaranteeing freedom of China while recognizing each other's special interests
Around the World Automobile Race ends in Paris
French chemist Eugène Schueller founds L'Oréal with his new range of hair dyes
John Heydler becomes president of baseball's National League
Conclusion of the Second Balkan War
French troops withdraw 10 km from the German border
German saboteurs blow up a munitions plant on Black Tom Island, New Jersey
Board of Commissioners of Cleveland Metroparks holds its first meeting
The Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA) forms; the party changes its name to the South African Communist Party (SACP) in 1953 after it is forced underground
New Zealand claims Ross Dependency in Antarctica
Albanian boundaries deduced
First broadcast of "Death Valley Days" on NBC Radio
First FIFA World Cup Final, Estadio Centenario, Montevideo, Uruguay: Uruguay beats Argentina 4-2 in the inaugural event
X Summer Olympic Games open in Los Angeles, California
Phillies' Dolph Camilli plays first base and registers no putouts
Soviet Politburo issues NKVD Order No. 00447 during the Great Purge to repress property-owning kulaks and anti-Soviets; 269,100 are arrested, with 76,000 executed
German occupiers forbid the political parties SDAP, VDB, ARP, RKSP, CHU, and SGP in the Netherlands
German occupiers set a night curfew on Jews in the Netherlands
German SS kills 25,000 Jews in Minsk, Belorussia
US 45th Infantry Division occupies San Stefano di Camastra in Sicily
WWII: US 30th division reaches the suburbs of Saint-Lô, Normandy with heavy battles also occurring at Tessy-sur-Vire and Villebaudon
First rocket attains 100 mi (167 km) altitude in White Sands, New Mexico
Cincinnati Reds pitcher Ewell Blackwell's record-breaking 16-game winning streak ends with a 5-4 loss to the New York Giants
Professional wrestling premieres on US prime-time TV (DuMont Television Network)
British warship HMS Amethyst escapes down the Yangtze River after being refused safe passage by Chinese Communists following a 3-month standoff
Bob Kennedy hits the first grand slam for the new Baltimore Orioles
Fifth British Empire Games and Commonwealth Games open in Vancouver, Canada
First AFL preseason game: Boston Patriots defeat Bills in Buffalo 28-7
South Vietnamese commandos attack North Vietnamese radar and military installations on Hòn Mê and Hòn Ngư Islands in the Gulf of Tonkin
MLB Milwaukee Brewers manager Bobby Bragan says his pitchers threw 75 to 80 spitballs in a 9-2 loss to the San Francisco Giants
Beatles' "Yesterday... and Today" album goes to #1 and stays at #1 for 5 weeks
FIFA World Cup Final, Wembley Stadium, London: Striker Geoff Hurst scores a hat trick as England beats West Germany 4-2 after extra time
US airplanes bomb the demilitarized zone between North and South Vietnam
Race riot in Milwaukee kills four
Washington shortstop Ron Hansen makes the eighth unassisted triple play in MLB history and the first in 41 years as the Senators lose 10-1 to the Cleveland Indians
Astros Denis Menke and Jim Wynn hit grand slams in the ninth inning against the Mets
KAEC TV channel 19 in Lufkin, Texas (ABC) begins broadcasting
30,000 people arrive to attend the Powder Ridge Rock Festival in Middlefield, Connecticut, despite its cancellation due to local residents' opposition
Hurricane Celia kills 31 in Cuba, Florida, and Texas
Riots hit Belfast, North Ireland
38th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Baltimore 24, All-Stars 17 (52,289 attendees)
All Nippon Airways Flight 58 collides with a Japan Air Self-Defense Force F-86F near Shizukuishi, Japan, killing all 162 aboard the Boeing 727
US Apollo 15 (Scott & Irwin) lands on Mare Imbrium on the Moon
Texas Rangers' Jim Bibby no-hits first-place Oakland 6-0
House Judiciary Committee votes on the third and final charge of "high crimes and misdemeanors" to impeach President Nixon in the Watergate cover-up
Murtala Mohammed overthrows General Gowon to become Nigerian Head of State
Simon Gray's "Otherwise Engaged" premieres in London
US Teamsters President Jimmy Hoffa disappears in suburban Detroit; Hoffa legally declared dead in 1982
Four Protestant civilians are shot dead at a pub off Milltown Road, Belfast; the attack is claimed by the Republican Action Force
Giulio Andreotti is sworn in as the premier of Italy for the second time
Japan defeats Russia for Olympic gold in woman's volleyball
Expos crush Braves 19-0, collecting 28 hits and tying the National League record with eight home runs
American swimmers Mary T. Meagher (2.06.37) and Craig Beardsley (1.58.21) set 200 m butterfly world records at the US National Swimming Championships at Irvine, California
Houston Astros pitcher J. R. Richard suffers a stroke and collapses while playing a game of catch before an Astros game, ending his career
Vanuatu (New Hebrides) gains independence from Britain and France
Belgian Senate accepts laws against racism
Simon Gray's play "Quartermaine's Terms" premieres in London
UN Human Rights Commission rules Canada violates the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights in the case of Wolastoqiyik woman Sandra Lovelace, who loses her status through marriage
Atlanta Braves remove Chief Noc-A-Homa to make room for more seating
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
Official speed record for a piston-driven aircraft: 832 km/h (517 mph) in California
Weightlifter Sergei Didyk of USSR jerks a record 261 kg
Alvenus tanker at Cameron, Louisiana, spills 2.8 million gallons of oil
Hollie Roffey, aged 10 days, is the youngest person to ever receive a heart transplant and survives 28 days
Soap Opera "Santa Barbara" premieres on NBC TV
Discovery moves to the Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center in Florida for the mating of the STS 51-I mission
Valerie Lowrance of Texas, 18, is crowned America's Junior Miss
An ANC car bomb directed at the headquarters of the Wits Command in Johannesburg, South Africa, kills one person and injures 68
ArenaBowl II takes place at Allstate Arena in Rosemont, Illinois: Detroit Drive defeats Chicago Bruisers 24-13, with Steve Griffin named MVP
Cincinnati Reds pitcher John Franco sets a record of 13 saves in 1 month
Harry Drake shoots an arrow a record 1,873 m
Jordanian King Hussein renounces sovereignty over West Bank to the PLO hours after dissolving Jordan's House of Representatives
MLB Pitcher John Franco saves a record 13th game during the month of July
Ronald J. Dossenbach begins a world-record ride, pedaling across Canada from Vancouver to Halifax in 13 days, 15 hours, and 4 minutes
Chile amends its constitution
Five Bank of Credit and Commerce members are found guilty of money laundering
Soldiers open fire on worshippers in Monrovian church, killing 200-600
The first Saturn automobile rolls off the assembly line
MTV announces it will split into three channels in 1993
Red Sox player Carlos Quintana becomes the 11th player to achieve six RBIs in an inning (3rd)
Lin Li swims a female world record in the 200m medley with a time of 2:11.65
Yael Arad becomes Israel's first Olympic medalist when she wins silver in the women's 61 kg judo in Barcelona; 20th anniversary of the Munich massacre and 500th anniversary of the Alhambra Decree
Record 103.8°F (39.9°C) in Preschen, Lusatia, Germany
Dominic Cork takes a hat-trick in England's Test cricket win against West Indies at Old Trafford
Eighteen lives are lost in the Thredbo landslide in New South Wales, Australia
Terrorist double suicide bombing in Jerusalem kills 14 people
Bruce Sutter, relief pitcher for the Cubs, Cardinals, and Braves, is inducted into the MLB Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, New York
The world's longest running music show "Top of the Pops" is broadcast for the last time on BBC Two after airing for 42 years
A bomb explodes in Palma Nova, Mallorca, killing two police officers. Basque separatist group ETA is believed to be responsible.
General Motors (Chevrolet) signs a record-breaking 7-year, $559 million sponsorship deal with EPL club Manchester United
Indian power grid failure leaves over 300 million without electricity
Train fire kills 32 and injures 27 people in Andhra Pradesh, India
134 people are killed in tribal conflicts in Darfur, Sudan
28 people are killed in a series of bombings in Kano, Nigeria
Mamnoon Hussain is elected President of Pakistan
Nine children are killed and 20 are injured after a school bus collides with a truck in Hanumangarh, India
Wikileaks discloser Bradley (later Chelsea) Manning is convicted of 17 espionage charges
Death toll in Gaza reaches 1,346, while 56 Israeli soldiers and 3 civilians are killed
The EU and US extend sanctions on Russia to include banks, energy, and defense firms; Moscow denies the allegation that Russia is arming rebels in Eastern Ukraine
In Bandar Mahshahr, Iran, the temperature reaches 46°C (115°F), but humidity makes it feel like 73°C (163°F)
Air balloon crashes into power lines near Lockhart, Texas, killing all 16 on board
Fifteen people killed in gunfights with Philippine police, including Reynaldo Parojinog, mayor of Ozamiz in an anti-drug trafficking operation
Hackers reveal they have stolen data from HBO, including episodes and scripts of Games of Thrones
Largest gathering of people with the same first name: 2,325 people named Ivan in Kupres, Bosnia and Herzegovina
Venezuelan voting for a new assembly is disrupted by violent protests, killing at least three people
Australian Catholic Archbishop Philip Wilson resigns after being convicted of concealing sexual abuse
British Foreign Secretary Jeremy Hunt mistakenly calls his Chinese-born wife "Japanese" during a meeting with his Chinese counterpart in Beijing
Four million people are excluded from the Assam National Register of Citizens in India, prompting fears of deportation and loss of citizenship
Federal law enforcement officers begin a phased withdrawal from Portland amid criticism of their actions to shut down Black Lives Matter protests
NASA launches its Perseverance rover and Ingenuity helicopter to Mars aboard an Atlas V 541 rocket from Cape Canaveral, Florida
The US economy posts the largest quarterly fall on record, with GDP down 9.5% for the 3 months to June 30
One out of 169 Americans is employed by Amazon in new figures released by the company, making it the US's second-largest employer, with Walmart being the largest, employing one in 100
Évian Championship Women's Golf, Évian Resort GC: Céline Boutier of France wins her first major title by 6 strokes over Canada's defending champion Brooke Henderson
Suspected suicide bomber kills 45 people and injures 135 at a rally for the religious political party Jamiat Ulema Islam in Khar, northwestern Pakistan
Ukraine's President VolodymyrZelenskyywarns war is coming back to Russia after a drones attack on Moscow
A mob of up to 300 riots in Southport, England, injuring 50 police officers, whipped up by misinformation on social media in the wake of a knife attack at a children's dance event
Hezbollah senior commander Fuad Shukr is killed in an Israeli airstrike in Beirut along with three others, injuring 74, in retaliation for a missile strike on the Golan Heights
Landslides in the Wayanad region of the Indian state of Kerala kill at least 172 people, with over 227 missing
Paul Dans resigns as head of Project 2025, a conservative group blueprint for a Republican presidency launched by the Heritage Foundation, amid criticism of its policies as authoritarian

8.8 magnitude earthquake strikes off Russia's far eastern Kamchatka Peninsula, triggering tsunami warnings across the Pacific Ocean; evacuations ordered in parts of Japan and Hawaii, as well as alerts being issued in China, New Zealand, the Philippines, and other island countries
First Australian-made rocket by Gilmour Space Technologies launches in Queensland, Australia, for 14 seconds before crashing
High-resolution images reveal intricate tattoos on a 2,500-year-old female Siberian 'ice mummy'
Klyuchevskoy Volcano in Russia's Kamchatka Peninsula erupts
Temperature of 41.2°C (106.2°F) in Tamba, Hyogo prefecture, Japan, sets new record for country, eclipsing previous high of 41.1°C set in 2018 and 2020; surpassed less than a week later
Battle of Vercellae: Roman army underGaius Mariusdefeats the Cimbri in Cisalpine Gaul, ending the Celto-Germanic threat on Italy's border with over 100,000 Cimbri killed
Battle of Alexandria:Mark Antonyachieves a minor victory overOctavian, but most of his army subsequently deserts, leading to Octavian's invasion of Egypt
Holy Roman EmperorFrederick IBarbarossa is crowned King of Burgundy
English Parliament sets KingCharles INewcastle Propositions
Johan de Wittis sworn in as the Grand Pensionary of Holland
CaptainJames Cookwith HMS Resolution safely returns to England at the end of his second voyage
FatherMiguel Hidalgo y Costilla, leader of the Mexican insurgency, is executed after being found guilty of treason by the Spanish military in Chihuahua, Mexico
Italian composerGioachino Rossinibecomes manager of Théâtre Italien in Paris, France
PresidentAbraham Lincolnissues "eye-for-an-eye" order to shoot a rebel prisoner for every Black prisoner shot
US Civil War, Siege of Petersburg: Union GeneralAmbrose Burnsidefails in an attack on Petersburg, Virginia, in the Battle of the Crater
NonpareilJack Dempseyfights George Fulljames in what is possibly the first middleweight fight with boxing gloves

After initial reluctance, TsarNicholas IIof Russia is persuaded to decree a general mobilization in response to Austria-Hungary's mobilization a few days earlier; "Think of the thousands and thousands of men who will be sent to their deaths!" he claims
John Frenchappointed Commander-in-Chief of the British Expeditionary Force
George Eastmanshows the first amateur color motion pictures to guests at his New York house, includingThomas Edison
Franklin D. Rooseveltsigns a bill creating the United States Naval Reserve (Women's Reserve), better known as WAVES
Czech distance running legendEmil Zátopekwins the 10,000 m at the London Olympics with an Olympic record of 29:59.6
Ty Cobbtestifies before the Emanuel Celler committee, denying that the reserve clause makes peons of baseball players
Ford Fricksets a waiver rule to bar inter-league deals until all clubs in the same league get the right to bid
Elvis Presleyjoins the Memphis Federation of Musicians Local 71
PresidentDwight D. Eisenhowersigns a law officially designating "In God We Trust" as the national motto of the United States
In his MLB debut, SF Giants future Baseball Hall of Fame first basemanWillie McCoveygoes 4-for-4 in a 7-2 win over Philadelphia Phillies at Seal Stadium
American singerBarbra Streisandopens for pianistLiberaceat the International Hotel in Las Vegas, Nevada
Soviet officials announce they have granted political asylum to British spyKim Philbyafter his escape to Moscow
Charles Ives' "From the Steeples and the Mountains" premieres at Lincoln Center with the New York Philharmonic more than 60 years after its composition
Duke Ellington's orchestral piece "The Golden Broom and the Green Apple" premieres with the New York Philharmonic led by Lukas Foss at the Lincoln Center in NYC
US PresidentLyndon Johnsonsigns Medicare bill, which goes into effect in 1966
George Harrisonreleases the single "Bangla Desh"
Future TV personality ("Keeping Up with the Kardashians") and transgender figure,Bruce Jenner(now Caitlin Jenner), sets a world record with 8,618 points to win decathlon gold at the Montreal Olympics
"I Just Want to Be Your Everything" byAndy Gibbreaches number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100
George Steinbrenneris forced by Commissioner of BaseballFay Vincentto resign as principal owner of the NY Yankees
Heavy metal bandMetallicarelease their single "Enter Sandman"
Richie AshburnandMike Schmidtenter baseball's Hall of Fame
Tommy Lasordaretires as LA Dodgers' manager
Columbia Records releasesBruce Springsteen's 12th studio album "The Rising," his first new record with the E Street Band since 1985, and it tops the charts in seven countries
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushsigns the "Sarbanes-Oxley Act" into law, legislation designed to prevent future accounting scandals such as Enron's bankruptcy

Toronto Rocks - SARSStock benefit with performers includingThe Rolling Stones,AC/DC, Rush, The Guess Who,Justin Timberlake, The Flaming Lips, and The Isley Brothers, at Downsview Park; largest concert in Canadian history attracts 450,000 spectators
"Harold & Kumar Go to White Castle", starringKal Pennand John Cho, is released
China's PresidentXi Jinpingheads a 12,000-troop parade at the Zhurihe training base in Inner Mongolia to commemorate 90 years since the founding of the People's Liberation Army
Russian PresidentVladimir Putinannounces American diplomats in Russia cut by 755 in response to American sanctions

Former presidentBarack Obamagives the eulogy at the funeral of Georgia congressman and civil rights leaderJohn Lewis; former presidentsGeorge W. BushandBill Clintonalso speak at Ebenezer Baptist Church in Atlanta
Ukrainian President VolodymyrZelenskyycalls for the mandatory evacuation of civilians from the eastern Donetsk region amid fierce fighting
Hamas political leaderIsmail Haniyehis assassinated in Iran's capital Tehran
City of Baghdad founded by Abbasid Caliph Al-Mansur, just north of ancient Baghdad
First Penguin Books published, starting the paperback revolution

After delivering the atomic bomb across the Pacific, cruiser USS Indianapolis is torpedoed and sunk by Japanese submarine I-58, killing 880 of the crew, many of whom are attacked by sharks, inspiring the movie "Jaws"
The last "old style" Volkswagen Beetle rolls off the assembly line in Mexico
First centenary of WWI Battle of Passchendaele commemorated at ceremony at Menin Gate in Ypres, Belgium
Official Malaysian investigation into the disappearance of Flight MH370 is unable to determine what happened
Giorgio Vasari, Italian painter and art historian (Vasari's Lives), born in Arezzo, Republic of Florence (d. 1574)
Ferdinando I de' Medici, Grand Duke of Tuscany, born in Florence (d. 1609)
Regnier de Graaf, Dutch physician and anatomist, born in Schoonhoven, Netherlands (d. 1673)
Stepan Fyodorovich Apraksin, Russian general who commanded Russian armies during the Seven Years' War, born in Riga, Russian Empire (d. 1758)
Antonio Benedetto Maria Puccini, Italian composer, born in Lucca, Republic of Lucca (d. 1832)
Maria Anna Mozart, Austrian pianist and Wolfgang's sister, born in Salzburg, Austrian Empire (d. 1829)
Samuel Rogers, English poet (Italy, a poem), born in Newington Cross, Islington, England (d. 1855)
Joseph-François Snel, Belgian composer, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1861)
Charles Chiniquy, Canadian Catholic priest who left the Roman Catholic Church and became a Presbyterian minister, born in Kamouraska, Quebec (d. 1899)
Thomas Jackson Rodman, American Brevet Brigadier General (Union Army), inventor of the rodman gun and perforated-cake gunpowder, born in Salem, Indiana (d. 1871)
John Heemskerk, Dutch politician (Minister of the Interior 1866-88), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1897)
Eugenio Terziani, Italian composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1889)
Chaim Aronson, Lithuanian inventor and academic, born in Seredžius, Lithuania (d. 1893)
Helena Blavatsky, Russian occultist, philosopher, and author (co-founded Theosophical Society), born in Yekaterinoslav, Ukraine (d. 1891)
Elon John Farnsworth, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Green Oak, Michigan (d. 1863)
Eugen Richter, German politician (Liberal), born in Düsseldorf, Germany (d. 1906)
James E. Kelly, American sculptor "Sculptor of American History", born in New York City (d. 1933)
Richard Haldane, British viscount and lord-chancellor (Life of A Smith), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1928)
Thorstein Veblen, American economist (Theory of the Leisure Class-1899), born in Cato, Wisconsin (d. 1929)
Henry Louis Smith, American physicist who made the first X-ray photograph, born in Greensboro, North Carolina (d. 1951)
Henry Lunn, English humanitarian and religious leader, born in Horncastle, Lincolnshire (d. 1939)
Alfred Weber, German economist and sociologist, born in Erfurt, Prussian Saxony (d. 1958)
Clémentine, Princess of Belgium, wife of Prince Napoleon Bonaparte V, born in Royal Castle of Laeken, Brussels, Belgium (d. 1955)
James W. Tate, English composer, born in Wolverhampton, Staffordshire, England (d. 1922)
Charles Radoux-Rogier, Belgian composer, born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1952)
Robert R. McCormick, American editor and publisher (Chicago Tribune), born in Chicago (d. 1955)
Smedley Butler, American Marine general, born in West Chester, Pennsylvania (d. 1940)
Holmes Herbert, British actor (Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde, The Kiss), born in Mansfield, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1956)
Leo van Puyfielde, Belgian art historian and museum curator (worked with the Monuments Men during WWII to recover looted artifacts), born in Sint-Niklaas, Belgium (d. 1965)
Algot Haquinius, Swedish pianist, and composer, born in Sveg, Sweden (d. 1966)
Felix Andries Vening Meinesz, Dutch geophysicist (gravity), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1966)
Raden Soetomo, Indonesian freedom fighter, born in Ngepeh, Nganjuk, Indonesia (d. 1938)
Vladimir Zworykin, Russian-American inventor of the cathode-ray tube for television, born in Murom, Russia (d. 1982)
Roy Park, Australian cricketer (one Test Aust v Eng 1920, out 1st ball), born in Charlton, Victoria (d. 1947)
Blanche Knopf, American publisher, president of Alfred A. Knopf, Inc. and wife of publisher Alfred A. Knopf Sr., born in New York City (d. 1966)
Carl Snavely, American College Football Hall of Fame coach (Bucknell, North Carolina, Cornell 180–96–16), born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1975)
Wanda Hawley, American silent screen actress (The Affairs of Anatol), born in Scranton, Pennsylvania (d. 1963)
Jacques de Kadt, Dutch politician and writer (Fascism is War!), born in Oss, Netherlands (d. 1988)
Henry Moore, English artist and sculptor (known for Three Piece Sculpture: Vertebrae), born in Castleford (d. 1986)
Gerald Moore, English pianist (Am I Too Loud), born in Watford, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1987)
Harry Moorman, Dutch vice-admiral (KVP), born in Roermond, Netherlands (d. 1971)
John Woods Duke, American composer, born in Cumberland, Maryland (d. 1984)
Alfred Lépine, Canadian ice hockey player and coach, born in Sainte-Anne-de-Bellevue, Canada (d. 1955)
Salvador Novo, Mexican writer and poet, born in Mexico City (d. 1974)
Ľudovít Rajter, Slovak composer and conductor, born in Pezinok, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 2000)
Serafin Pro, Cuban composer, born in La Habana, Cuba (d. 1977)
C. Northcote Parkinson, English historian (Pursuit of Progress), born in Barnard Castle, County Durham, England (d. 1993)
Edgar de Evia, Mexican-American photographer, born in Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico (d. 2003)
Ivo Lhotka-Kalinski, Yugoslav composer, born in Zagreb, Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia (d. 1987)
Karl Guttmann, Austrian-Dutch playwright and director (Albee/Pinter), born in Bielsko-Biała, Poland (d. 1995)
Michael Morris, 3rd Baron Killanin, Irish journalist, author, sports official and 6th President of the International Olympic Committee (1972-80), born in London, England (d. 1999)
Dick Wilson, British-American actor, born in Preston, Lancashire, England (d. 2007)
Robert van Spaendonck, Dutch resistance fighter, born in Tilburg, Netherlands (d. 1944)
Eddy Grove, American actor (Dragnet, The Silent Service, Medic), born in New York City (d. 1995)
Joe Daley, American jazz tenor, clarinet and flute player, born in Salem, Ohio (d. 1994)
Berniece Baker Miracle, American writer (My Sister Marilyn) and half-sister of Marilyn Monroe, born in Venice Beach, California (d. 2014)
George Lampe, Dutch painter and director (Free Academy), born in Schiedam, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Grant Johannesen, American concert pianist, born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 2005)
Jacques Van der Schueren, Belgian minister of Economic Affairs, born in Aalst, Belgium (d. 1997)
Henry W. Bloch, American businessman (co-founder of H&R Block) and philanthropist, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2019)
Zbigniew Wiszniewski, Polish composer, born in Lwów, Lwowskie, Poland (d. 1999)
Dipa Nusantara Aidit, Indonesian communist leader of PKI (1951-65), born in Belitung Island, Dutch East Indies (d. 1965)
Siegfried Köhler, German conductor, music director, and composer, born in Freiburg im Breisgau, Germany (d. 2017)
C. T. Vivian, American civil rights activist, worked with Martin Luther King, born in Boonville, Missouri (d. 2020)
Christopher Shaw, British composer, chiefly of choral music (Peter and the Lame Man), born in London (d. 1995)
William H. Gass, American novelist and philosopher (Omensetter's Luck), born in Fargo, North Dakota (d. 2017)
Antoine Duhamel, French composer, born in Valmondois, Val-d'Oise, France (d. 2014)
Jacques Sernas [Jokūbas Bernardas Šernas], Lithuanian actor (La Dolce Vita, Helen of Troy), born in Kaunas, Lithuania (d. 2015)
Joe George, American character actor (Across 110th Street; Seinfeld - "Uncle Mac"), born in Massachusetts (d. 1992)
Richard Johnson, British stage and screen actor and producer (Never So Few, Restless, Beyond the Door), born in Upminster (d. 2015)
Victor Wong [Yee Keung Victor Wong], American actor (Big Trouble in Little China), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2001)
Eunice Muñoz, Portuguese actress (A Morgadinha dos Canaviais; Hard Times), born in Amareleja, Portugal (d. 2022)
Joe Nuxhall, American baseball pitcher and sportscaster (youngest MLB player at 15 years, 316 days), born in Hamilton, Ohio (d. 2007)
Valentin Muratov, Russian gymnast (4 gold, 1 silver medals 1952, 1956 Olympics), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 2006)
Christine McGuire, American pop vocalist (McGuire Sisters - "Sincerely"; "Sugartime"), born in Middletown, Ohio (d. 2018)
Sid Krofft, Canadian puppeteer and television producer - with his brother Marty (H.R. Pufnstuf; Land of the Lost; Sigmund and the Sea Monsters; Donny & Marie), born in Montreal, Quebec
Bobby Dimond, Australian rugby league winger (24 Tests; NSW, Western Suburbs), born in Dapto, Australia (d. 2020)
Tony Lip [Frank Anthony Vallelonga], American author and actor (Sopranos, Donnie Brasco, Good Fellas), born in Beaver Falls, Pennsylvania (d. 2013)
Frederick Swann, American church and concert pipe organist (Riverside Church, NYC, 1952-82; Robert Shuller's Crystal Cathedral Hour of Power), born in Lewisburg, West Virginia (d. 2022)
Joan Vohs, American model and actress (Fort Ti, Vice Squad, Sabrina), born in St Albans, New York (d. 2001)
Edd Byrnes, American actor (77 Sunset Strip, Jack the Ripper), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2020)
Ben Piazza, American actor (Santa Barbara; The Blues Brothers; Dallas), born in Little Rock, Arkansas (d. 1991)
André Prévost, Canadian composer, born in Hawkesbury, Ontario, Canada (d. 2001)
Bud Selig, American Baseball Hall of Fame team owner (Milwaukee Brewers) and executive (MLB Commissioner 1998-2015), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin
Kirsten Simonsen, Danish prima ballerina, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2024)[1]
William Negri, Italian soccer goalkeeper (12 caps; Mantova, Bologna), born in Bagnolo San Vito, Italy (d. 2020)
(George) "Buddy" Guy, American blues guitarist (Stone Crazy), born in Lettsworth, Louisiana
Infanta Pilar of Spain, Duchess of Badajoz, daughter of Infante Juan, Count of Barcelona and Princess María Mercedes of Bourbon-Two Sicilies, born in Cannes, France (d. 2020)
István Zelenka, Hungarian-Austrian-Swiss contemporary classical and electro-acoustic composer (Vivat Nucleus), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2022)
Ralph Taeger, American actor (Klondike, Acapulco, Hondo), born in Richmond Hill, New York (d. 2015)
Reva Rose, American stage and screen actress (You're A Good Man, Charlie Brown; Temperature's Rising), born in Chicago, Illinois
